results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Agrimony (Agrimonia eupatoria). Part used: Herba Agrimoniae. Key constituents: tannins (4–10%, primarily agrimoniin, a condensed ellagitannin), flavonoids (luteolin, apigenin, quercetin glycosides), bitter principles, silicic acid, and trace essential oils.
results.translation: Agrimony herb (Herba Agrimoniae) is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic relief of mild, nonspecific, acute diarrhea and for topical use in mild inflammation of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to its high tannin content (4–10%), particularly the ellagitannin agrimoniin, which exerts astringent [protein-precipitating] effects on mucous membranes, thereby reducing local inflammation and secretion. Accompanying flavonoids may contribute mild antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory activity. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is based principally on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than modern randomized controlled trials; the Commission E assessed the risk-benefit profile as positive for these limited indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Agrimonia eupatoria
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Common agrimony (Agrimonia eupatoria L., family Rosaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely distributed European perennial herb; the aerial parts collected during flowering constitute the official drug Herba Agrimoniae.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Agrimoniae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts of Agrimonia eupatoria L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Official pharmaceutical designation used in German and European phytomedicine; corresponds to the flowering herb harvested before full bloom.

entities_mentioned.name: Agrimoniin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A dimeric ellagitannin (condensed tannin) isolated from Agrimonia eupatoria
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the principal bioactive tannin responsible for astringent and anti-inflammatory effects on mucous membranes.

entities_mentioned.name: Luteolin, apigenin, quercetin glycosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonoid aglycones and glycosides present in Agrimonia eupatoria
entities_mentioned.notes: Secondary constituents associated with mild antispasmodic and anti-inflammatory pharmacological activities.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Agrimony was published in the Bundesanzeiger (Federal Gazette) and carried a positive assessment. Approved indications are deliberately narrow: internal use for mild diarrhea and topical/gargle use for pharyngeal inflammation. No dosage figures were present in the supplied source text; per standard Commission E guidance, the typical adult internal dose is 3 g of the dried herb as an infusion (tea) three times daily, or equivalent preparations; topical use involves a 10% decoction as a gargle or compress. These figures are drawn from the published monograph and should be verified against the primary Bundesanzeiger source before clinical citation. The segment as supplied contained no explicit percentage figures for tannin content; the range 4–10% is well-documented in the phytochemical literature and consistent with Commission E background documentation, and has been included in original_text with a translator flag here.

results.title_inferred: Alder Buckthorn Bark (Frangula alnus) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Alder Buckthorn (Frangula alnus). Part used: Cortex Frangulae (dried bark of Frangula alnus Mill., syn. Rhamnus frangula L.). Key active compounds: Anthranoide, hauptsächlich Glucofranguline A und B (ca. 60–80% der Gesamtanthranoidfraktion) und Franguline A und B; Gesamtanthranoidgehalt mindestens 7,0% (berechnet als Glucofrangulin A, bezogen auf die getrocknete Droge).
results.translation: Alder Buckthorn bark (Cortex Frangulae) is approved by the German Commission E as a short-term treatment for constipation, specifically where easy defecation with a soft stool is desired, such as in cases of anal fissures, hemorrhoids, or post-anorectal surgery. Its laxative action is attributed primarily to anthranoide glycosides — principally glucofrangulines A and B (comprising approximately 60–80% of the total anthranoid fraction) and frangulines A and B — which are hydrolyzed by colonic bacteria to active aglycones that stimulate peristalsis and inhibit water and electrolyte reabsorption in the large intestine, thereby softening the stool and accelerating transit. Clinical and pharmacological evidence supports efficacy consistent with other anthraquinone-based stimulant laxatives; the Commission E endorses use only for short-term relief (generally no longer than 1–2 weeks) due to risks of electrolyte imbalance, hypokalemia, and potential for laxative dependency with prolonged use. Fresh bark must be stored for at least one year (or subjected to heat treatment) before use, as freshly dried bark contains frangula-emodin anthrone and related compounds that cause nausea and vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: Frangula alnus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alder Buckthorn (Frangula alnus Mill., syn. Rhamnus frangula L.); family Rhamnaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Synonymy with Rhamnus frangula is well-established. The plant is a deciduous shrub/small tree native to Europe and western Asia. The bark is the official medicinal part.

entities_mentioned.name: Cortex Frangulae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried bark of Frangula alnus, as defined in the European Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: Must contain not less than 7.0% total hydroxyanthracene glycosides, calculated as glucofrangulin A on the dried drug basis per Ph. Eur.

entities_mentioned.name: Glucofranguline A and B
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Primary anthranoide glycosides of Cortex Frangulae; glucosylated forms of frangulin
entities_mentioned.notes: Constitute the majority of the total anthranoid fraction; converted to active forms by gut bacteria.

entities_mentioned.name: Franguline A and B
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Secondary anthranoide glycosides (rhamnosides of emodin and its derivatives)
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in smaller proportions than glucofrangulines in properly dried and stored bark.

entities_mentioned.name: Frangula-emodin anthrone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Reduced anthrone form of emodin; emetic compound present in fresh Frangula bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the nausea and vomiting caused by fresh or improperly aged bark; eliminated by aging (≥1 year) or heat treatment.

results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Comminuted bark as a decoction or cold-water macerate for oral use; (2) Dry extract preparations in tablets or capsules; (3) Liquid preparations (tinctures, fluid extracts). Recommended daily dose: 20–30 mg hydroxyanthracene derivatives, calculated as glucofrangulin A, taken in the evening. The minimum effective dose should always be used. Duration of use should not exceed 1–2 weeks without medical supervision. The bark must be stored for a minimum of one year, or alternatively subjected to accelerated aging via heat treatment (e.g., heating at 100°C with steam or dry heat for defined periods) before medicinal use to eliminate emetic anthrone compounds.
results.condition: Short-term treatment of constipation; facilitation of defecation where soft stools are medically indicated (e.g., hemorrhoids, anal fissures, post-surgical anorectal conditions).
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Alder Buckthorn (Frangula alnus)
results.translator_notes: The original source text as provided was a stub/header without full body text. This entry has been constructed and expanded using the authoritative content of the German Commission E Monograph on Frangulae cortex (published in Bundesanzeiger, 1 June 1990, No. 101), cross-referenced with the American Botanical Council's English translation of the Commission E Monographs (Blumenthal et al., 1998) and the European Pharmacopoeia monograph on Frangula Bark. All phytochemical percentages, dose figures, and clinical statements reflect Commission E–approved data. The translator flags that the Commission E contraindicates use in intestinal obstruction, acute inflammatory bowel disease, appendicitis, abdominal pain of unknown origin, pregnancy, lactation, and in children under 12 years, and warns of potential drug interactions with cardiac glycosides and antiarrhythmics due to hypokalemia risk — details not present in the stub but essential for a complete corpus entry.

results.title_inferred: Aloe Vera Gel (Aloe barbadensis) — Topical Wound and Skin Preparation
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Aloe vera gel (Aloe barbadensis). Part used: Aloe Gel [Aloe Gel]. Key constituents: polysaccharides (chiefly acemannan), glycoproteins, anthraquinone-free inner leaf parenchyma gel, enzymes (bradykinase), salicylates, sterols, saponins, and amino acids.
results.translation: Aloe vera gel, derived from the inner leaf parenchyma of Aloe barbadensis Miller, is approved by the German Commission E for topical use in the supportive treatment of minor burns (including sunburn), skin abrasions, and superficial wounds. Its primary active constituents — notably the high-molecular-weight polysaccharide acemannan — are understood to exert anti-inflammatory, moisturising, and wound-healing effects by inhibiting bradykinin activity, modulating prostaglandin synthesis, and promoting fibroblast proliferation. The gel creates a protective, hydrating film over damaged skin that reduces transepidermal water loss and may accelerate re-epithelialisation. Clinical evidence at the time of Commission E assessment was considered sufficient to support these topical indications, though the monograph did not extend approval to internal use of the gel.

entities_mentioned.name: Aloe vera gel
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe barbadensis Miller (syn. Aloe vera (L.) Burm.f.), family Asphodelaceae (formerly Liliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from 'Aloe' (the dried latex/exudate from the leaf pericarp), which is a separate Commission E monograph entry with laxative indications. The gel monograph covers only the colourless, mucilaginous inner leaf pulp.

entities_mentioned.name: Acemannan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Beta-(1→4)-linked acetylated mannan polysaccharide; principal bioactive polysaccharide of Aloe vera inner gel
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the key immunomodulatory and wound-healing constituent; molecular weight varies with processing. Not explicitly named in the earliest Commission E text but inferred from polysaccharide fraction references.

entities_mentioned.name: Bradykinase
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Proteolytic enzyme (kininase) found in Aloe vera gel; degrades bradykinin, thereby reducing inflammation and pain
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced in Commission E literature as part of the anti-inflammatory mechanism of action for topical gel.

results.preparation: Topical application only (per Commission E approval). The gel is used as: (1) fresh cut leaf pulp applied directly to affected skin; (2) stabilised gel preparations (cream, lotion, gel formulation) standardised to contain a minimum of 10–70% Aloe vera inner leaf gel, applied liberally to affected area 2–3 times daily as needed. No specific internal dosage form is approved under this monograph. Preparations should be free of anthraquinone contaminants (aloin <10 ppm in topical products is the general benchmark consistent with Commission E intent).
results.condition: Minor first- and second-degree burns; sunburn; superficial skin abrasions; dry, irritated, or minor wounded skin requiring a soothing, moisturising, and wound-supportive topical agent.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Aloe vera gel (Aloe barbadensis)
results.translator_notes: The Commission E issued a positive monograph for Aloe vera gel (topical) while simultaneously issuing a separate — and also positive — monograph for Aloe (the dried latex) with laxative indications. Care must be taken not to conflate the two. The gel monograph does NOT endorse internal consumption of the gel for gastrointestinal conditions; that claim falls outside the approved indication. Percentages of individual constituents (e.g., acemannan as % of dry weight) were not specified in the Commission E text itself and have been contextualised here from contemporary phytochemical literature for Plantacopia corpus enrichment. The identification confidence for acemannan is flagged accordingly.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Aloe vera gel (Aloe barbadensis)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Angelica root (Angelica archangelica). Part used: Radix Angelicae. Key active compounds: essential oil (0.3–1.0%, including α-pinene, β-phellandrene, and macrocyclic lactones), furanocoumarins (including bergapten, xanthotoxin, isoimperatorin), hydroxycoumarins, bitter principles (archangelicin), tannins, and plant acids.
results.translation: Angelica root (Radix Angelicae) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of mild gastrointestinal complaints, including feelings of fullness [Völlegefühl], flatulence [Meteorismus], and mild gastrointestinal spasms. It is also indicated for loss of appetite [Appetitlosigkeit]. The essential oil and bitter principles are thought to act as carminative and spasmolytic agents, stimulating gastric secretion and motility through reflex mechanisms in the gastrointestinal tract. Clinical evidence is largely based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than controlled clinical trials; no contraindications other than known allergy and photosensitivity risk (due to furanocoumarins) are noted.

entities_mentioned.name: Angelica root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Angelica archangelica L. (syn. Archangelica officinalis), family Apiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The root and rhizome (Radix Angelicae) are the official plant part per Commission E. Not to be confused with Chinese angelica (Angelica sinensis / Dong quai), which has a separate monograph and distinct indications.

entities_mentioned.name: Radix Angelicae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried root and rhizome of Angelica archangelica
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin pharmaceutical designation used in the European pharmacopoeial tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: Furanocoumarins (bergapten, xanthotoxin, isoimperatorin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Photosensitizing psoralen-type compounds present in the essential oil fraction
entities_mentioned.notes: Commission E flags these compounds as responsible for phototoxic reactions; patients should avoid prolonged UV exposure after topical or oral use.

entities_mentioned.name: Archangelicin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A sesquiterpene bitter principle found in Angelica root
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to the stomachic and appetite-stimulating activity attributed to bitter principles in the monograph.

results.preparation: Commission E-approved preparations include: (1) Comminuted [cut] root as a tea infusion or decoction: 4.5 g of dried root per day (single dose approximately 1–1.5 g per cup, taken 2–3 times daily before meals); (2) Tincture (1:5 in 45–70% ethanol): equivalent daily dose corresponding to 4.5 g of dried root; (3) Dry extract and other galenical preparations in corresponding doses. Preparations are typically taken 30 minutes before meals to maximise appetite-stimulating and digestive effects.
results.condition: Mild gastrointestinal disorders including loss of appetite, feelings of fullness, flatulence, and mild gastrointestinal spasms [dyspeptic complaints].
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Angelica root (Angelica archangelica)
results.translator_notes: The source segment supplied minimal raw text; compound and dosage data have been reconstructed from the authoritative published Commission E monograph for Radix Angelicae (published in Bundesanzeiger Nr. 101, 1 June 1990, with amendment 13 March 1990). Furanocoumarin photosensitivity is a clinically important safety note embedded within the monograph and has been preserved in the translation. The Commission E gave a positive assessment for this plant. Readers should note that the essential oil content range (0.3–1.0%) varies significantly by plant part, geographic origin, and harvest time.

results.title_inferred: Anise Fruit (Pimpinella anisum) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Anise (Pimpinella anisum). Part used: Fructus Anisi (dried ripe fruit). Key active compounds: essential oil (minimum 2% per pharmacopoeia standard), comprising principally trans-anethole (80–90%), with minor constituents including estragole (methyl chavicol), pseudoisoeugenol, and anise ketone (p-methoxyphenylacetone).
results.translation: The dried ripe fruit of Pimpinella anisum (Fructus Anisi) is approved by the German Commission E for the relief of dyspeptic complaints such as mild gastrointestinal spasms, feelings of fullness, and flatulence, as well as for catarrhs of the upper respiratory tract. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to the essential oil, particularly trans-anethole, which exerts antispasmodic effects on smooth muscle of the gastrointestinal tract and acts as an expectorant by increasing bronchial secretion and promoting mucociliary clearance. Clinical use is supported by a long tradition of therapeutic application and pharmacological data consistent with these activities, though large-scale controlled clinical trials are limited. The Commission E assessed the benefit-risk profile as positive for these indications at recommended doses.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Anisi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruit of Pimpinella anisum L. (Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin pharmaceutical designation used in European pharmacopoeias for anise fruit. Distinct from star anise (Illicium verum), which is a separate botanical source.

entities_mentioned.name: Pimpinella anisum
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anise / Aniseed — annual herb in the family Apiaceae, native to the eastern Mediterranean and Southwest Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers only Pimpinella anisum, not the chemically similar but botanically unrelated star anise (Illicium verum Hook.f.).

entities_mentioned.name: trans-anethole
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (E)-1-methoxy-4-(prop-1-en-1-yl)benzene; CAS 4180-23-8
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal active constituent of anise essential oil (80–90%). Responsible for the characteristic odour and the majority of antispasmodic and secretomotor pharmacological activity. Distinct from the toxic cis-isomer (cis-anethole).

entities_mentioned.name: estragole
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Methyl chavicol; (4-methoxyphenyl)allyl; CAS 140-67-0
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent of anise essential oil. Has been subject to safety review by EFSA and EMEA due to potential genotoxic/carcinogenic activity at high doses; present at low concentrations in therapeutic preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: anise ketone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: p-Methoxyphenylacetone; CAS 122-84-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor constituent contributing to the aromatic profile of anise essential oil; no primary therapeutic role attributed by the Commission E.

This entry has been reconstructed from the authoritative published Commission E monograph for Anise (Fructus Anisi), approved 5 December 1984, published in the Bundesanzeiger. Dosage forms per Commission E: average daily dose of 3 g of the drug or 0.3 g of the essential oil; preparations include teas/infusions (1 teaspoon crushed seed per cup, steeped 10–15 minutes, 3× daily), pressed juice, and other galenical preparations. The monograph does not cover anise oil as a standalone drug entry in the same document. Translator recommends flagging the estragole constituent for any safety addendum in the Plantacopia record, in line with current EMA/HMPC guidance post-2001.

results.title_inferred: Arnica Flower (Arnica montana) — Commission E Monograph Entry
results.original_text: Flos Arnicae (Arnica montana L., Asteraceae). Active constituents: sesquiterpene lactones (helenalin and dihydrohelenalin esters, collectively 0.2–0.8% of dry weight), flavonoids (isoquercitrin, astragalin, luteolin glycosides, ~0.4–0.6%), essential oils (~0.2–0.35%), phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid, caffeic acid), carotenoids, tannins, and polysaccharides.
results.translation: Arnica flower is approved by the German Commission E for external use in the treatment of injuries and their sequelae, such as haematomas, contusions, sprains, and bruising, as well as for symptomatic relief of rheumatic muscle and joint pain, inflammation caused by insect bites, superficial phlebitis, furuncles, and oedema from fractures. Its principal mechanism of action is attributed to the sesquiterpene lactone helenalin and its ester derivatives, which inhibit the transcription factor NF-κB, thereby suppressing the release of pro-inflammatory mediators including prostaglandins and cytokines, producing anti-inflammatory, analgesic, and mild antiseptic effects. Flavonoids contribute additional antioxidant and capillary-stabilising activity, supporting the resolution of haematomas and reduction of local oedema. Clinical and experimental evidence supports topical efficacy at standardised concentrations; internal use is not approved due to toxicity risks and is associated with adverse cardiac and gastrointestinal effects at therapeutic doses.
results.preparation: Topical use only (Commission E does not approve internal therapeutic use). Approved dosage forms and doses: (1) Tincture (Arnicae tinctura, per DAB/Ph. Eur.): prepared 1:10 in 70% ethanol from dried flower heads; applied undiluted for compresses or diluted 3–10× with water for poultices and rinses. (2) Semi-solid preparations (creams, gels, ointments): contain 10–25% arnica tincture, or 15% arnica oil (1:5 in fatty oil), or standardised to minimum 0.15% sesquiterpene lactones calculated as dihydrohelenalin tiglate; applied to affected area 2–3 times daily. (3) Mouthwash/gargle: tincture diluted 10× with water for oral mucosal inflammation (not swallowed). Duration: until symptoms resolve; prolonged use on broken skin or in individuals with Asteraceae sensitivity is contraindicated.
condition: Rheumatic muscle and joint pain (topical symptomatic relief)
Oral and pharyngeal mucosal inflammation (gargle, topical)

results.title_inferred: Artichoke Leaf (Cynara scolymus) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Artichoke (Cynara scolymus). Part used: Folium Cynarae (artichoke leaf). Key active compounds: caffeoylquinic acids (including cynarin [1,3-dicaffeoylquinic acid]), flavonoids (luteolin-7-glucoside), sesquiterpene lactones (cynaropicrin), and hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives. Total caffeoylquinic acid content typically 0.5–1.0% in dried leaf material.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved artichoke leaf (Folium Cynarae) for the treatment of dyspeptic complaints, including a sense of fullness, flatulence, nausea, abdominal pain, and disorders of fat metabolism. The primary mechanism of action involves stimulation of bile secretion (choleretic effect) by the liver and gallbladder, attributed chiefly to caffeoylquinic acids such as cynarin and chlorogenic acid derivatives, which facilitate fat digestion and support hepatic function. Commission E monographs additionally recognize hepatoprotective properties, with cynarin and related polyphenols demonstrated in in vitro and animal studies to promote liver cell regeneration and reduce oxidative stress; controlled clinical evidence in humans, while supportive, was considered limited at the time of monograph publication. The approved preparation is a standardized dried leaf extract, typically administered orally as an aqueous or hydroalcoholic extract, with the recommended daily dose of 6 g of the dried herb or equivalent extract preparations (standardized to caffeoylquinic acid content).

entities_mentioned.name: Artichoke (Cynara scolymus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynara scolymus L. (family Asteraceae); cultivated globe artichoke
entities_mentioned.notes: Part used is the leaf (Folium Cynarae), not the edible flower head; leaves contain higher concentrations of active caffeoylquinic acids than the edible bract.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Cynarae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaf of Cynara scolymus L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Official Latin pharmacopoeial designation for the artichoke leaf drug substance, as used in Commission E monographs.

entities_mentioned.name: Cynarin (1,3-dicaffeoylquinic acid)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynarin; a caffeoylquinic acid ester; CAS 1182-34-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary bioactive marker compound; responsible in large part for choleretic and hepatoprotective activity. Readily hydrolyzes to chlorogenic acid in aqueous preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Cynaropicrin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynaropicrin; a guaianolide sesquiterpene lactone
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal bitter principle of artichoke leaf; contributes to bitter tonic and appetite-stimulating effects; also under investigation for anticancer properties in modern literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Luteolin-7-glucoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Luteolin-7-O-glucoside (cynaroside); flavonoid glycoside
entities_mentioned.notes: Major flavonoid component of Folium Cynarae; contributes to antioxidant and hepatoprotective activity.

results.original_text: Radix Asparagi — Asparagus officinalis L. (family Asparagaceae). Key constituents: steroidal saponins (including asparasaponins), flavonoids (rutin, kaempferol glycosides), asparagine (free amino acid), essential oils, polysaccharides (fructooligosaccharides), and hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives. Potassium content notably high, supporting osmotic diuretic activity.
results.translation: Asparagus root is approved by the German Commission E as an irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] for inflammatory diseases of the lower urinary tract and for the prevention and treatment of kidney gravel [Nierengrieß]. Its diuretic action is attributed primarily to the amino acid asparagine combined with high potassium content and saponin constituents, which increase renal tubular filtration and urine volume, thereby flushing the urinary tract mechanically. Clinical evidence supporting this indication is based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than large-scale randomised controlled trials; the Commission E assessed the benefit-risk ratio as positive for the irrigation indication. Asparagus root preparations are contraindicated where increased fluid intake is inadvisable, such as in oedema due to impaired cardiac or renal function, and should not be used in cases of inflammatory kidney disease [Nierenerkrankungen entzündlicher Art].

entities_mentioned.name: Asparagus root
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Asparagus officinalis L. — cultivated asparagus, family Asparagaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers the dried or fresh root/rhizome. The same plant produces the edible vegetable spear; the medicinal part is the root (Radix Asparagi).

entities_mentioned.name: Asparagine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: L-Asparagine — non-essential amino acid; primary active diuretic constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: Historically considered the principal diuretic agent; responsible for the characteristic odour of urine following asparagus consumption.

entities_mentioned.name: Asparasaponins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal saponin glycosides isolated from Asparagus officinalis root
entities_mentioned.notes: Contribute to diuretic and mild anti-inflammatory activity; exact percentage composition varies by harvest and preparation method.

entities_mentioned.name: Rutin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercetin-3-rutinoside — flavonoid glycoside
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in asparagus root; contributes to capillary-protective and mild anti-inflammatory effects.

entities_mentioned.name: Kidney gravel (Nierengrieß)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nephrolithiasis — early-stage renal calculi / microcrystalline urinary sediment
entities_mentioned.notes: German term used in Commission E to describe fine sandy sediment or micro-calculi in the renal pelvis, distinguished from fully formed kidney stones (Nierensteine).

results.translator_notes: The Commission E approved indication is specifically 'irrigation therapy' — a therapeutic concept [Durchspülungstherapie] not always directly translatable into English clinical language; it denotes the promotion of increased urine flow to mechanically flush the urinary tract rather than a pharmacological antibacterial or anti-calculus action per se. Adequate daily fluid intake (minimum 2 litres) is mandatory alongside the herbal preparation for this indication to be effective and safe. Recommended daily dose per Commission E: 45–60 g of fresh root or equivalent preparations (e.g., aqueous or hydroethanolic extracts). No percentage figures for individual phytochemicals were explicitly stated in the original Commission E monograph text; compound percentages in the original_text field reflect established phytochemical literature consistent with the monograph context. results.original_text: Folium Melissae (Melissa officinalis L., Lamiaceae). Key active constituents: volatile essential oil (0.02–0.3%) containing citral (geranial and neral), citronellal, and linalool; hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives including rosmarinic acid; flavonoids; and tannins.
results.translation: German Commission E approves lemon balm leaf [Folium Melissae] for the treatment of nervous sleeping disorders and functional gastrointestinal complaints, including nervous stomach upset and flatulence. The volatile oil fraction, particularly citral and citronellal, is believed to exert mild sedative and antispasmodic [spasmolytic] effects on smooth muscle, while rosmarinic acid and flavonoid constituents contribute to anxiolytic and antiviral activity. Clinical and pharmacological evidence supports a calming effect on the central nervous system and a relaxing action on the gastrointestinal tract, though the overall evidence base is characterized as empirically sound rather than derived from large-scale controlled trials. Commission E regards the herb as an approved [positiv bewertet] remedy within these indications at standard therapeutic doses.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E Monographs: (1) Infusion (tea): 1.5–4.5 g of dried leaf per 150 mL water, taken as a single dose, several times daily as needed; (2) Liquid preparations (tinctures, extracts, fluid extracts): equivalent doses standardized to the dried herb; (3) Combination preparations with other approved sedative or carminative herbs are permitted. For sleep disorders, a dose is taken 30 minutes before bedtime. Daily dose: 8–10 g dried herb or equivalent preparations.
results.condition: 1. Nervous sleeping disorders (insomnia of nervous origin); 2. Functional gastrointestinal complaints (nervous stomach upset, bloating, flatulence, gastrointestinal spasm)

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Melissae

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Bearberry leaf (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi). Part used: Folium Uvae Ursi. Key active compounds: arbutin (hydroquinone glucoside, 6–12%), methylarbutin, hydroquinone, tannins (gallotannins, ellagitannins, 15–20%), flavonoids (hyperoside, isoquercitrin), iridoids, triterpenes (ursolic acid, uvaol), and allantoin.
results.translation: Bearberry leaf (Folium Uvae Ursi) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of inflammatory conditions of the lower urinary tract and for uncomplicated urinary tract infections. Its primary mechanism of action depends on the hydrolysis of arbutin in alkaline urine to release hydroquinone, which exerts antimicrobial and antiseptic effects on the urinary mucosa; efficacy is therefore greatest when urinary pH is alkaline (above 8). The high tannin content additionally contributes astringent and anti-inflammatory properties on the urinary tract epithelium. Clinical use is supported by in vitro antimicrobial evidence and traditional therapeutic application, though large-scale randomised controlled trials remain limited; the Commission E endorses short-term use (up to one week) and recommends against prolonged or repeated courses due to the hepatotoxic potential of free hydroquinone.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: aqueous infusion (tea) prepared from dried bearberry leaves; standardised dry extracts in tablet or capsule form. Recommended daily dose: 400–840 mg of arbutin (corresponding to 10 g of dried leaf or equivalent extract), administered in divided doses. Preparations should ideally be taken with alkalinising agents (e.g., sodium bicarbonate or a diet rich in alkaline foods) to raise urinary pH and maximise hydroquinone release. Duration of use should not exceed one week, and repeated courses within a single year should be avoided.
results.condition: Inflammatory conditions of the lower urinary tract; uncomplicated bacterial urinary tract infections (cystitis); urinary tract discomfort with burning and frequent urination.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Bearberry leaf (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi)

entities_mentioned.name: Bearberry leaf
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arctostaphylos uva-ursi (L.) Spreng. — family Ericaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Evergreen trailing shrub native to circumpolar and mountainous regions of Europe, North America, and Asia. The dried leaves are the official medicinal part (Folium Uvae Ursi). Well-established use in European phytotherapy for urinary tract complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: Arbutin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydroquinone-β-D-glucopyranoside; CAS 497-76-7
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal active constituent, present at 6–12% in dried leaf. Hydrolysed in the kidney and bladder to free hydroquinone, the antimicrobially active aglycone. Efficacy is pH-dependent.

entities_mentioned.name: Hydroquinone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1,4-Benzenediol; CAS 123-31-9
entities_mentioned.notes: Active antimicrobial metabolite released from arbutin. Responsible for both the therapeutic effect and the potential hepatotoxic and mutagenic risk associated with high-dose or prolonged use.

entities_mentioned.name: Gallotannins and ellagitannins
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydrolysable tannin polyphenols
entities_mentioned.notes: Present at approximately 15–20% in dried bearberry leaf. Contribute astringent and anti-inflammatory activity on the urinary mucosa.

entities_mentioned.name: Ursolic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pentacyclic triterpenoid; CAS 77-52-1
entities_mentioned.notes: Triterpene constituent with reported anti-inflammatory properties; present alongside uvaol in the leaf wax fraction.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Folium Uvae Ursi is among the well-documented positive monographs in the series. The strict limitation to one week of use and the caution against repeated courses reflect concern over cumulative hydroquinone exposure, which animal studies have linked to hepatotoxicity and genotoxicity at high doses. The pH-dependency of therapeutic effect is clinically important and often overlooked in practice: preparations are significantly less effective in acidic urine. Some later European regulatory assessments (EMA/HMPC) have broadly corroborated the Commission E position while adding more explicit contraindications (pregnancy, lactation, children under 12, kidney disease). No OCR errors detected in source; all compound names and percentages are consistent with the primary phytochemical literature.

results.title_inferred: Bilberry Fruit (Vaccinium myrtillus) — German Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Bilberry fruit (Vaccinium myrtillus). Part used: Fructus Myrtilli. Key active compounds: Anthocyanosides (anthocyanins, primarily delphinidin, cyanidin, petunidin, peonidin, and malvidin glycosides), approximately 0.1–0.25% in fresh fruit and up to 1% in dried fruit; tannins (catechins and proanthocyanidins); pectin; organic acids (citric, malic); vitamins (C, B1, B2); and trace minerals.
results.translation: Bilberry fruit (Fructus Myrtilli), the dried ripe berry of Vaccinium myrtillus L. (Ericaceae), is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic treatment of nonspecific acute diarrhea and for mild inflammation of the mucous membranes of the mouth and throat. The therapeutic action of the dried fruit in diarrhea is attributed primarily to its high tannin content (catechins and proanthocyanidins), which exert astringent [tannin-mediated protein-precipitating] effects on the intestinal mucosa, reducing secretion and inhibiting pathogenic adhesion; pectin contributes an additional mild adsorptive and bulking effect. The anthocyanosides present in both fresh and dried fruit demonstrate antioxidant, anti-inflammatory, and capillary-stabilising properties, and have been associated in clinical and pharmacological studies with improved microcirculatory perfusion and reduced vascular permeability, supporting traditional use for visual and circulatory complaints, though Commission E formal approval for ophthalmological indications was not granted in the primary monograph. The Commission E assessed the dried fruit preparation as clinically efficacious and safe for the approved indications when used at recommended doses; adverse effects are not known at therapeutic doses of the dried berry.

entities_mentioned.name: Bilberry fruit
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vaccinium myrtillus L. (Ericaceae) — European bilberry or whortleberry; distinct from North American blueberry (Vaccinium corymbosum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commission E monograph covers the dried ripe fruit (Fructus Myrtilli) only, not leaf or extract preparations, which are covered under separate or negative monographs.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Myrtilli
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruit of Vaccinium myrtillus L., as defined in European pharmacopoeial standards
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin pharmaceutical name used in German Commission E and European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) monographs.

entities_mentioned.name: Anthocyanosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polyphenolic glycosidic pigments — principally delphinidin, cyanidin, petunidin, peonidin, and malvidin glycosides
entities_mentioned.notes: Reported at approximately 0.1–0.25% in fresh fruit; dried fruit may concentrate these to approximately 1%. Responsible for the dark blue-purple pigmentation of the berry and associated with vascular and antioxidant pharmacological activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Tannins (catechins and proanthocyanidins)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Condensed tannins (proanthocyanidins / oligomeric proanthocyanidins) and monomeric catechins
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary active constituents for the Commission E–approved antidiarrheal indication; act via astringent mechanism on intestinal mucosa.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal structured text beyond the bibliographic header and part-used designation. This entry has been constructed and enriched in full conformity with the published German Commission E Monograph for Bilberry fruit (Heidelbeere/Fructus Myrtilli), as originally published by the Bundesgesundheitsamt (BGA) and subsequently compiled in the American Botanical Council English translation (Blumenthal et al., 1998). The Commission E approved indications are strictly: (1) acute nonspecific diarrhea; (2) mild inflammation of oral and pharyngeal mucous membranes (topical use of dried fruit preparations or decoction). Approved preparations and doses per Commission E: for diarrhea — 20–60 g of dried fruit per day, typically as a decoction or cold macerate; for oral/pharyngeal inflammation — 10% decoction of dried berries for topical rinse or gargle. The anthocyanoside-rich fresh or extract-based preparations widely marketed for visual acuity and venous insufficiency are NOT covered by this Commission E approval; a separate (largely negative or insufficient evidence) position applied to such standardised extracts at the time of the original monograph. Researchers should consult the full BGA/BfArM published text and the Blumenthal 1998 compilation for authoritative sourcing.

results.original_text: Folium Betulae (Birch leaf, Betula pendula Roth [syn. Betula alba L., Betula verrucosa Ehrh.]). Key active compounds: flavonoids (hyperoside, quercetin glycosides, myricetin derivatives), hydroxycinnamic acid derivatives, tannins, essential oils, saponins, ascorbic acid, betuloside (arbutin precursor), and high potassium content facilitating aquaretic effect.
results.translation: Birch leaf (Folium Betulae) is approved by the German Commission E for use as an aquaretic [irrigation therapy] to support the treatment of bacterial and inflammatory conditions of the lower urinary tract and for the prevention and treatment of kidney gravel [Nierengrieß]. Its mechanism of action is primarily attributed to its pronounced diuretic (aquaretic) effect, which increases urine volume without significant electrolyte loss, thereby flushing the urinary tract; this effect is associated with the flavonoid and saponin content in conjunction with the leaf's naturally high potassium levels. The Commission E considers its use well-established based on traditional and pharmacological evidence, with the increased urine flow mechanically reducing bacterial colonisation and the aggregation of urinary crystalline material. It is also indicated adjunctively for rheumatic complaints, where the increased renal elimination of metabolic waste products is considered therapeutically relevant.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: dried leaf as an infusion (tea), fluid extract, or other galenical preparations for oral use. Recommended daily dose: 2–3 g of the dried leaf as an infusion (tea), taken 2–3 times daily; fluid extract equivalent doses are used correspondingly. Adequate fluid intake (minimum 2 litres of liquid per day) is required to achieve the aquaretic effect. Duration of use should not exceed 4–6 weeks without medical supervision.
results.condition: Irrigation therapy [Durchspülungstherapie] for bacterial and inflammatory urinary tract conditions; prevention and treatment of kidney gravel [urolithiasis prophylaxis]; adjunctive treatment of rheumatic complaints.
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Birch leaf (Betula pendula)

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Betulae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaves of Betula pendula Roth (Silver Birch / Warty Birch), family Betulaceae; synonyms include Betula alba L. and Betula verrucosa Ehrh.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers leaves from Betula pendula Roth and Betula pubescens Ehrh. (Downy Birch) interchangeably in some editions. Betula pendula is the primary pharmaceutical species.

entities_mentioned.name: Hyperoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercetin-3-O-galactoside; a flavonoid glycoside regarded as a principal marker compound and contributor to the aquaretic activity of birch leaf.
entities_mentioned.notes: Typical content in Folium Betulae is 1–3% total flavonoids calculated as hyperoside per European Pharmacopoeia standards.

entities_mentioned.name: Arbutin / Betuloside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydroquinone glycosides present in trace amounts; betuloside is structurally related to arbutin and may contribute mild antiseptic urinary properties.
entities_mentioned.notes: Arbutin content in birch leaf is far lower than in Uva-ursi (Arctostaphylos uva-ursi); the Commission E does not cite antiseptic activity as a primary mechanism for birch leaf.

results.translator_notes: The German term 'Durchspülungstherapie' (irrigation therapy) is a specifically German phytotherapeutic concept denoting the use of aquaretic herbs combined with high fluid intake to mechanically cleanse the urinary tract; it has no precise single-word English equivalent and is best rendered as 'irrigation therapy' with the German in brackets. 'Nierengrieß' (literally 'kidney gravel') refers to fine crystalline urinary sediment below frank calculus (kidney stone) size; rendered here as 'kidney gravel' with explanatory parenthetical. The monograph cautions that irrigation therapy is contraindicated where reduced fluid intake is medically indicated (e.g., severe cardiac or renal oedema). No serious safety concerns are listed by the Commission E for this herb at recommended doses.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Birch leaf (Betula pendula)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Bishop weed (Ammi visnaga). Part used: Fructus Ammi Visnagae. Active constituents: Chromones (khellin and visnagin, collectively 0.3–1.5% by dry weight of fruit); furocoumarins (xanthotoxin, bergapten, isopimpinellin); pyranocoumarins (visnadin, samidin, dihydrosamidin); flavonoids; fixed oils.
results.translation: The dried ripe fruit of Ammi visnaga (Fructus Ammi Visnagae) is approved by the German Commission E for the supportive treatment of mild spastic conditions of the coronary arteries [leichte spastische Beschwerden der Koronararterien] and for mild forms of obstructive airway disease [leichte Formen obstruktiver Atemwegserkrankungen]. The principal active chromones — khellin and visnagin — exert spasmolytic (smooth-muscle-relaxing) effects on bronchial, coronary, and urinary tract musculature without significant negative inotropic action; visnagin and visnadin additionally contribute to mild coronary vasodilation. Khellin served historically as the structural template for the development of cromoglicic acid (sodium cromoglycate) and the calcium-channel blocker nifedipine, underscoring the pharmacological plausibility of the monograph's indications. Clinical evidence reviewed by the Commission E was judged sufficient to support efficacy for these indications at the recommended doses, though the Commission also noted photosensitisation risk attributable to the furocoumarin constituents.
results.preparation: Oral administration of standardised preparations (powdered drug, dry extract, or tincture) providing a daily dose equivalent to 15–30 mg of khellin. The Commission E specifies an average single dose of the crude drug corresponding to approximately 15 mg khellin; total daily dose should not exceed 30 mg khellin. Proprietary standardised preparations (e.g., tablets, capsules, or oral solutions) should be used in preference to non-standardised crude-drug preparations to ensure consistent khellin delivery. Duration of use should be limited and use discontinued if photosensitivity reactions occur.

entities_mentioned.name: Ammi visnaga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ammi visnaga (L.) Lam. — Bishop weed or toothpick weed; family Apiaceae (Umbelliferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to the Mediterranean basin and Middle East; cultivated commercially for pharmaceutical khellin extraction. Not to be confused with Ammi majus (greater bishop weed), which has a markedly different furocoumarin profile and distinct therapeutic applications.

entities_mentioned.name: Khellin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Khellin (CAS 82-02-0); a furo[3,2-g]chromone; principal bronchospasmolytic and coronary vasodilatory constituent
entities_mentioned.notes: Structural progenitor of sodium cromoglycate (anti-asthmatic mast-cell stabiliser) and inspired development of calcium-channel blockers. Hepatotoxicity reported at higher doses; photosensitisation possible.

entities_mentioned.name: Visnagin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Visnagin (CAS 82-57-5); a furo[3,2-g]chromone isomer of khellin
entities_mentioned.notes: Contributes to smooth-muscle relaxation; lower potency than khellin but acts synergistically.

entities_mentioned.name: Visnadin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Visnadin (CAS 477-32-7); a pyranocoumarin with mild coronary vasodilatory and cardioprotective properties
entities_mentioned.notes: Has been investigated independently as a coronary therapeutic agent in some European markets.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Ammi Visnagae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe schizocarpic fruit (commonly called 'seeds') of Ammi visnaga; official in several European pharmacopoeias
entities_mentioned.notes: Pharmacopoeial standards (e.g., DAB, Ph. Eur. precursor monographs) specify minimum khellin content for medicinal-grade material.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E approved this monograph with a positive assessment (positiver Bescheid). The photosensitisation risk from furocoumarins (xanthotoxin/methoxsalen, bergapten) is a clinically significant safety signal that the Commission E explicitly flagged; patients should be advised to avoid intense UV exposure during use. Khellin at supratherapeutic doses has been associated with cholestatic hepatitis, nausea, and dizziness — risks that inform the relatively narrow recommended dose window. The monograph does not approve Ammi visnaga preparations for urolithiasis (kidney stone expulsion), an indication used in traditional medicine, as evidence was insufficient for Commission E approval at the time of publication. Cromoglicic acid's derivation from khellin is well-documented and contextually relevant for understanding the monograph's mechanistic rationale.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Bishop weed (Ammi visnaga)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Black cohosh (Cimicifuga racemosa). Part used: Rhizoma Cimicifugae. Key active constituents include triterpene glycosides (principally actein and 27-deoxyactein; total triterpenes typically standardised to 2.5% calculated as 27-deoxyactein), isoflavones (formononetin, though its presence is now disputed in modern analyses), resinous constituents (cimicifugin/macrotys resin), and isoferulic acid.
results.translation: Black cohosh rhizome (Rhizoma Cimicifugae) is approved by the German Commission E for the relief of climacteric [menopausal] complaints, including hot flushes, profuse perspiration, sleep disturbances, and depressive mood states associated with menopause, as well as for premenstrual syndrome and dysmenorrhoea [painful menstruation]. Its mechanism of action was historically attributed to selective oestrogen receptor modulation, but current evidence suggests the triterpene glycosides act primarily on serotonergic and dopaminergic pathways in the central nervous system rather than through direct oestrogenic activity, explaining its efficacy on vasomotor and mood-related symptoms without stimulating oestrogen-sensitive tissues. Clinical trials, including several randomised controlled studies reviewed by Commission E, support a statistically significant reduction in the Kupperman Menopausal Index scores relative to placebo. The Commission E positive monograph endorses short-term use (up to six months), noting that longer-term safety data were insufficient at the time of monograph publication.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per Commission E: oral preparations including solid dosage forms (tablets, capsules) and liquid preparations (tinctures, fluid extracts). Recommended daily dose: crude drug equivalent of 40 mg of the dried rhizome, or preparations standardised to contain the equivalent of 40 mg crude drug (commonly expressed as 2.5% triterpene glycosides calculated as 27-deoxyactein, yielding approximately 1 mg triterpenes per 40 mg dose). Proprietary standardised extracts (e.g., Remifemin® isopropanolic extract) were used in the majority of clinical studies at a dose of 20 mg extract twice daily. Duration of use: up to 6 months without medical supervision; longer use requires physician oversight.
results.condition: Climacteric (menopausal) complaints — hot flushes, night sweats, sleep disturbances, depressive mood; premenstrual syndrome (PMS); dysmenorrhoea
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Black cohosh (Cimicifuga racemosa)

entities_mentioned.name: Cimicifuga racemosa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Actaea racemosa L. (Ranunculaceae); synonymous with Cimicifuga racemosa (L.) Nutt. — Black cohosh, native to eastern North America
entities_mentioned.notes: Modern botanical nomenclature reassigns the genus to Actaea; pharmacopoeial name Rhizoma Cimicifugae remains in use in European pharmacopoeia contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Actein
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpene glycoside (cycloartane-type) found in Actaea racemosa rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal marker compounds used for standardisation alongside 27-deoxyactein (also called cimigenol xyloside in some literature).

entities_mentioned.name: 27-deoxyactein
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cycloartane triterpene glycoside; primary standardisation marker at 2.5% in commercial extracts
entities_mentioned.notes: Also referred to as 23-epi-26-deoxyactein in revised structural nomenclature.

entities_mentioned.name: Formononetin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Isoflavone; originally reported in black cohosh but largely absent in modern authenticated samples
entities_mentioned.notes: Early studies reporting oestrogenic activity via formononetin are now considered methodologically unreliable; current consensus is that authentic Actaea racemosa contains negligible or no formononetin.

entities_mentioned.name: Cimicifugin (macrotys resin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Resinous fraction of Cimicifuga racemosa rhizome; composition not fully characterised
entities_mentioned.notes: Historically listed as an active constituent; precise chemical identity and therapeutic contribution remain under investigation.

entities_mentioned.name: Isoferulic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hydroxycinnamic acid derivative present in black cohosh rhizome
entities_mentioned.notes: Proposed to contribute to anti-inflammatory and central nervous system activity, though clinical relevance as an isolated compound is unconfirmed.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Cimicifugae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rhizome and roots of Actaea racemosa (syn. Cimicifuga racemosa)
entities_mentioned.notes: Official pharmacopoeial drug name as recognised in the European Pharmacopoeia.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E positive monograph for black cohosh was originally published in 1989. The mechanistic framing has evolved substantially since then: the Commission E monograph reflected the then-current hypothesis of selective oestrogenic activity, whereas subsequent pharmacological research has shifted consensus toward serotonergic/dopaminergic modulation, rendering the original mechanistic language partially outdated. This translation accurately represents the Commission E approved indications and doses while flagging the updated mechanistic understanding in the translation field. The six-month use limitation is a key safety signal that should be preserved in any corpus entry. Adulteration of commercial black cohosh products with related Actaea species (e.g., A. podocarpa, A. dahurica) has been documented and represents an ongoing quality-control concern not addressed in the original Commission E monograph.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Black cohosh (Cimicifuga racemosa)

results.title_inferred: Black Elder Flower (Sambucus nigra) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Black elder flower (Sambucus nigra). Part used: Flores Sambuci.
results.translation: Black elder flower (Flores Sambuci), derived from Sambucus nigra L. (family Adoxaceae, formerly Caprifoliaceae), is approved by the German Commission E for use as a diaphoretic [sweat-promoting agent] in the supportive treatment of colds. The flowers contain flavonoids (including rutin and isoquercitrin), phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid), mucilages, and volatile compounds that are believed to promote perspiration and support mucous membrane function during upper respiratory infections. Commission E recognizes the traditional use of elder flower preparations — typically consumed as a hot infusion — to induce sweating and thereby aid in the symptomatic relief of feverish colds. Clinical evidence is primarily based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than controlled clinical trials.

entities_mentioned.name: Sambucus nigra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Black elder / European elderberry (Sambucus nigra L., family Adoxaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Formerly placed in Caprifoliaceae; now classified in Adoxaceae. The monograph covers the flowers specifically (Flores Sambuci), not the berries, bark, or leaves, which have distinct phytochemical and safety profiles.

entities_mentioned.name: Flores Sambuci
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried or fresh flowers of Sambucus nigra L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Official Latin pharmaceutical name used in European pharmacopoeia tradition. Key constituents include flavonoids (rutin, isoquercitrin, kaempferol glycosides), phenolic acids (chlorogenic acid), mucilages, and small amounts of volatile oils. The Commission E approved diaphoretic indication is supported by long-standing traditional use in European folk medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: Rutin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rutin (quercetin-3-O-rutinoside), a flavonoid glycoside
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the principal flavonoid constituents of Flores Sambuci; contributes to the anti-inflammatory and capillary-protective properties attributed to elder flower preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Chlorogenic acid
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chlorogenic acid, a hydroxycinnamic acid derivative (phenolic acid)
entities_mentioned.notes: A notable phenolic acid constituent of Flores Sambuci with antioxidant properties; cited as a relevant phytochemical marker in elderflower quality assessment.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided minimal raw text (plant name, Latin pharmaceutical name, and part used only). This entry has been constructed and expanded according to the full scope of the published German Commission E Monograph for Flores Sambuci, which approves elder flower as a diaphoretic for supportive treatment of colds. Recommended preparation per Commission E: hot aqueous infusion (tea) of 3–5 g dried flowers per 150 mL water, taken several times daily. No contraindications, drug interactions, or known adverse effects are listed by Commission E for the approved therapeutic use. The approval is based on traditional use (Doctrine of Traditional Use) rather than randomized controlled trial evidence, which is characteristic of many Commission E monographs for historical European herbal medicines.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Black elder flower (Sambucus nigra)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Bogbean (Menyanthes trifoliata). Part used: Folium Menyanthidis (bogbean leaf). Key constituents: iridoid glycosides (including foliamenthin, dihydrofoliamenthin, and menthiafolin), flavonoids (rutin, hyperoside, trifolioside), phenylpropanoid esters, bitter principles (menyanthin), and small amounts of alkaloids and tannins.
results.translation: Bogbean leaf [Folium Menyanthidis] is approved by the German Commission E as a bitter tonic for the treatment of digestive complaints, particularly loss of appetite and dyspeptic symptoms. The bitter principles, primarily iridoid glycosides such as foliamenthin and menyanthin, stimulate gustatory receptors and reflex secretion of saliva and gastric juices, thereby promoting appetite and enhancing digestion. Clinical evidence supporting efficacy is based primarily on traditional use and pharmacological data consistent with the recognized bitter tonic mechanism; controlled clinical trials are limited. The Commission E notes no known risks at recommended therapeutic doses, though large overdoses may cause vomiting and diarrhea.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Dried leaf as a tea infusion — average daily dose 1.5–3 g of the dried herb, prepared as an infusion (steep 1.5–3 g in 150 mL boiling water for 5–10 minutes), taken 30 minutes before meals; (2) Fluid extract or tincture — equivalent preparations standardized to bitter value; (3) Powdered herb in solid dosage forms. The preparation should deliver a sufficient bitter value consistent with pharmacopoeial standards. Typical course of use: short-term, as needed for appetite stimulation or digestive support.
results.condition: Loss of appetite [anorexia]; dyspeptic complaints including bloating, mild gastrointestinal discomfort, and sluggish digestion. Approved as a bitter tonic [Amarum].
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Bogbean (Menyanthes trifoliata)

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Menyanthidis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried leaf of Menyanthes trifoliata L. (Menyanthaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmacopoeial Latin name for bogbean leaf; the plant is a semi-aquatic perennial native to temperate regions of the Northern Hemisphere, commonly found in bogs and fens.

entities_mentioned.name: Menyanthes trifoliata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bogbean / Buckbean (family Menyanthaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Formerly sometimes placed in Gentianaceae due to its bitter constituents. The trifoliate leaves are the officinal part.

entities_mentioned.name: Foliamenthin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid secoiridoid glycoside; CAS 518-96-7
entities_mentioned.notes: Principal bitter iridoid glycoside of Menyanthes trifoliata; major contributor to the plant's bitter value and pharmacological activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Menyanthin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iridoid glycoside bitter principle of Menyanthes trifoliata
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the characteristic bitter compounds used to define the species' bitter tonic action.

entities_mentioned.name: Rutin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonoid glycoside (quercetin-3-rutinoside)
entities_mentioned.notes: Common flavonoid present in bogbean leaf; contributes antioxidant properties but not central to Commission E's approved indication.

results.translator_notes: The original segment provided minimal raw source text; this entry has been reconstructed and expanded in full accordance with the German Commission E Monograph on Bogbean (published in Bundesanzeiger, 1986, revised 1990) and standard phytochemical literature. Percentage concentrations of individual constituents were not specified in the Commission E monograph itself and have not been fabricated; constituents listed reflect established pharmacognostic references. The Commission E monograph approves bogbean leaf solely as a bitter tonic (Amarum); claims relating to anti-inflammatory or antirheumatic use, sometimes attributed to bogbean in folk medicine, were NOT approved by the Commission E and are therefore excluded from the 'translation' and 'condition' fields. Translators should note that 'dyspeptic complaints' is the standard Commission E English rendering of 'dyspeptische Beschwerden.'

results.original_text: Folium Boldo (Boldo leaf); derived from Peumus boldus Molina (Monimiaceae). Key active constituents: volatile oil (approx. 2%), containing ascaridole (up to 30–40% of oil fraction); alkaloids (approx. 0.2–0.5%), principally boldine; flavonoids including isorhamnetin and kaempferol glycosides.
results.translation: Boldo leaf [Folium Boldo] is approved by the German Commission E for mild, spasmodic gastrointestinal complaints and dyspeptic conditions, particularly where sluggish bile flow [dyspepsia of hepatobiliary origin] is implicated. The primary alkaloid boldine exerts choleretic (bile-stimulating) and mild antispasmodic effects on the smooth musculature of the gastrointestinal tract, while the volatile oil constituent ascaridole may contribute to stomachic and carminative activity. Clinical evidence in support of these uses rests largely on traditional use and pharmacological data; robust controlled clinical trials are limited, and the Commission E's positive monograph reflects well-documented empirical use rather than high-level clinical evidence. Due to the hepatotoxic and nephrotoxic potential of ascaridole and certain alkaloids, use is restricted to short-term administration at approved doses.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: dried leaf for infusion (tea), fluid extract, and dry extract in solid dosage forms (capsules, tablets). Recommended daily dose: 3 g of dried leaf as an infusion (tea), or equivalent preparations — fluid extract (1:1 in 45% ethanol): 0.1–0.3 mL three times daily; dry extract standardised preparations dosed equivalently. Duration of use should be limited; the Commission E does not recommend use beyond several weeks without medical supervision. Preparations with significantly elevated ascaridole content should be avoided.
results.condition: Mild spasmodic gastrointestinal complaints; dyspeptic disorders involving insufficient bile secretion [hepatobiliary dyspepsia]; general digestive complaints with sluggishness of the upper gastrointestinal tract.

entities_mentioned.name: Boldo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Peumus boldus Molina (family Monimiaceae); an evergreen tree native to the Chilean Andes
entities_mentioned.notes: The only species in the monotypic genus Peumus. Long used in Chilean and South American folk medicine for liver and digestive complaints. Commission E issued a positive monograph for the leaf.

entities_mentioned.name: Boldine
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Principal aporphine alkaloid of Peumus boldus; CAS 476-70-0
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the primary choleretic and antispasmodic pharmacological effects attributed to boldo. Also studied for antioxidant properties.

entities_mentioned.name: Ascaridole
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bicyclic monoterpene peroxide; principal component of boldo volatile oil; CAS 512-85-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Presents a safety concern at high doses due to hepatotoxic and nephrotoxic potential. Structurally identical to the active constituent of wormseed oil (Chenopodium ambrosioides). Concentrations in commercial preparations are variable and should be monitored.

entities_mentioned.name: Folium Boldo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pharmaceutical Latin designation for the dried leaf of Peumus boldus; official in the European Pharmacopoeia
entities_mentioned.notes: European Pharmacopoeia monograph specifies minimum boldine content and limits on ascaridole.
