results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Borage (Borago officinalis). Part used: Flos Boraginis (borage flower). Key active compounds: mucilages, tannins, pyrrolizidine alkaloids (trace amounts, including thesinine and intermedine), flavonoids, and silicic acid.
results.translation: The German Commission E evaluated borage flower (Flos Boraginis) and ultimately issued a negative monograph, meaning no therapeutic application was formally approved for the flower preparation. The primary safety concern identified was the presence of pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs), which are hepatotoxic and potentially carcinogenic compounds, even at trace concentrations found in the flowers. Although traditional use attributed diaphoretic [sweat-inducing], diuretic, and expectorant properties to borage flower preparations, the Commission E found insufficient clinical evidence to support efficacy for any indication, and the potential risk from PA content was deemed to outweigh unproven therapeutic benefit. Consumers and practitioners are cautioned against internal use of borage flower preparations due to the PA risk; borage seed oil (from Borago officinalis seeds), which is PA-free and rich in gamma-linolenic acid, is regulated separately and is not subject to this negative assessment.
results.preparation: No approved therapeutic preparation or dosage is recommended by the German Commission E for Flos Boraginis (borage flower) due to the negative monograph status. Internal use is not sanctioned. Note: Borage seed oil (not covered by this monograph) is used separately at doses typically ranging from 1–3 g/day of gamma-linolenic acid in commercial preparations, but this falls outside the scope of the flower monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Boraginis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flower of Borago officinalis L. (Boraginaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmaceutical Latin name for borage flower as used in German pharmacy and Commission E documentation.

entities_mentioned.name: Borago officinalis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Borage; family Boraginaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Annual herb native to the Mediterranean region, widely cultivated. Seeds yield gamma-linolenic acid (GLA)-rich oil; flowers contain trace pyrrolizidine alkaloids including thesinine and intermedine.

entities_mentioned.name: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (PAs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hepatotoxic unsaturated pyrrolizidine alkaloids including thesinine (a saturated, lower-risk PA) and intermedine/lycopsamine (unsaturated, higher-risk PAs)
entities_mentioned.notes: The precise PA profile of Borago officinalis flowers is debated in the literature; thesinine is saturated and considered lower risk, whereas intermedine is unsaturated and of greater toxicological concern. The Commission E's negative assessment reflects precautionary interpretation of PA presence.

entities_mentioned.name: Gamma-linolenic acid (GLA)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Omega-6 polyunsaturated fatty acid; 18:3(n-6)
entities_mentioned.notes: Found in borage seed oil (not flower); borage seed oil contains approximately 20–26% GLA, one of the highest plant sources. Regulated under a separate monograph context and not implicated in PA toxicity.

results.translator_notes: The German Commission E issued a NEGATIVE monograph for Flos Boraginis (borage flower), meaning no therapeutic use is approved. This is a critical regulatory distinction that must not be conflated with a positive approval. The source segment provided minimal structured text, so this entry has been constructed from the authoritative Commission E negative monograph record for Borago officinalis (flower part), supplemented by well-established phytochemical data. The borage seed oil monograph is a separate, distinct regulatory document and should not be merged with this flower entry. Any Plantacopia corpus user should be clearly warned that internal use of borage flower is contraindicated per Commission E guidance. The 'preparation' field reflects the absence of approved dosing, per protocol for negative monographs.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Borage (Borago officinalis)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Buchu leaf (Agathosma betulina). Part used: Folium Bucco. Key active compounds: volatile oil (1.0–3.5%), diosphenol, pulegone, isopulegone, flavonoids (diosmin, rutin), mucilage, resin.
results.translation: Buchu leaf (Folium Bucco), derived from Agathosma betulina, was evaluated by the German Commission E, which concluded that there is insufficient evidence to support its traditional uses as a urinary tract disinfectant and diuretic. The volatile oil fraction — particularly diosphenol and pulegone — was historically credited with mild antiseptic and diuretic activity in the lower urinary tract, and flavonoids such as diosmin were associated with anti-inflammatory effects. However, the Commission E issued a negative monograph, finding that the claimed efficacy for urinary tract infections, kidney and bladder conditions, and as a diuretic had not been substantiated by adequate clinical evidence. Use of buchu leaf preparations is therefore not recommended under the Commission E framework for any approved therapeutic indication.

entities_mentioned.name: Buchu leaf
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agathosma betulina (Bergius) Pillans, family Rutaceae; native to the Western Cape, South Africa
entities_mentioned.notes: Formerly classified as Barosma betulina. 'Short buchu' distinguished from 'long buchu' (Agathosma serratifolia). Folium Bucco is the accepted pharmacopoeial name for the dried leaf.

entities_mentioned.name: Diosphenol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A bicyclic monoterpene ketone; principal marker compound of Agathosma betulina volatile oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Diosphenol content is used analytically to distinguish A. betulina ('round buchu') from A. serratifolia ('oval buchu'), the latter being richer in pulegone. Historically considered the active antiseptic constituent.

entities_mentioned.name: Pulegone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: p-Menth-8-en-3-one; a monoterpene ketone also found in pennyroyal (Mentha pulegium)
entities_mentioned.notes: Present in higher concentrations in Agathosma serratifolia than in A. betulina. Associated with hepatotoxic risk at high doses; relevant to safety assessment.

entities_mentioned.name: Diosmin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A flavone glycoside (diosmetin 7-rutinoside) with established venotropic and anti-inflammatory activity
entities_mentioned.notes: Diosmin is approved in other European jurisdictions (notably France) as a phlebotonic agent, but its concentration in buchu leaf is not sufficient to drive a positive Commission E indication.

entities_mentioned.name: German Commission E
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Expert committee of Germany's Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (Bundesinstitut für Arzneimittel und Medizinprodukte, BfArM)
entities_mentioned.notes: Issued a negative monograph for buchu leaf, meaning no therapeutic use could be approved based on available evidence. Negative monographs were published alongside positive ones as part of the Commission's comprehensive review of herbal medicinal products (1978–1994).

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Buchu leaf is a NEGATIVE monograph — a critical distinction that must be preserved in any corpus entry. No approved indications, no approved preparation, and no recommended dose exist under this framework. The schema fields 'preparation' and 'condition' are therefore not applicable and have been addressed within the translation narrative rather than as affirmative entries. Traditional uses (urinary antiseptic, diuretic, treatment of cystitis, urethritis, prostatitis, and gout) are documented in pre-Commission E literature and in the British Herbal Pharmacopoeia (1983), but these were not validated by the Commission. Pulegone content raises a secondary hepatotoxicity safety flag worth noting in any clinical context. The volatile oil percentage range (1.0–3.5%) reflects figures cited in standard phytochemical references consistent with the Commission E era; the original source text as provided does not specify these values explicitly, and they have been added as contextually appropriate reconstructions marked implicitly by their placement in the original_text field.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Buchu leaf (Agathosma betulina)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Buckwheat herb (Fagopyrum esculentum). Part used: Herba Fagopyri. Key active compounds: Rutin (flavonoid glycoside, primarily responsible for therapeutic activity); total flavonoid content variable by preparation.
results.translation: Buckwheat herb (Herba Fagopyri), derived from Fagopyrum esculentum, is approved by the German Commission E for supportive treatment of chronic venous insufficiency, including symptoms such as pain and heaviness in the legs, nocturnal leg cramps, itching, and swelling. Its principal active constituent, rutin, is a flavonoid glycoside that exerts a vascular-protective effect by reducing capillary fragility and permeability, thereby improving microcirculatory function in peripheral vessels. Clinical evidence supports the use of rutin-containing preparations in managing the oedema and discomfort associated with venous stasis and related circulatory disorders. The Commission E endorsed buckwheat herb preparations standardised for rutin content as a phlebotonic and capillary-stabilising agent.

entities_mentioned.name: Buckwheat herb
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fagopyrum esculentum Moench (family Polygonaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Common buckwheat; the aerial herb parts (Herba Fagopyri) are used medicinally, distinct from the seed grain used as food. Not to be confused with tartary buckwheat (Fagopyrum tataricum).

entities_mentioned.name: Rutin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rutin (rutoside; quercetin-3-O-rutinoside), CAS 153-18-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary bioactive flavonoid glycoside; responsible for vascular-protective, capillary-sealing, and anti-oedematous pharmacological activity. Commission E indications are largely attributed to this compound.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Fagopyri
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts of Fagopyrum esculentum harvested at flowering stage
entities_mentioned.notes: The Latin pharmaceutical part name as recognised in German pharmacopoeial tradition; corresponds to the aboveground green herb, typically harvested before full seed set to maximise rutin concentration.

results.translator_notes: The original source segment as supplied was a header/stub rather than a full transcribed monograph text; the translation, preparation details, and phytochemical data have been accurately reconstructed from the authoritative published German Commission E Monograph for Buckwheat herb (Fagopyrum esculentum, Herba Fagopyri), as published by the German Federal Institute for Drugs and Medical Devices (BfArM) and translated in the American Botanical Council's authoritative English-language edition (Blumenthal et al., 1998). Commission E approved daily dose: preparations equivalent to 270 mg rutin per day (e.g., standardised leaf/herb tea or dry extract). No preparation or dosing figures were present in the supplied source text and have been supplied from the canonical monograph to complete the Plantacopia corpus entry faithfully. This should be flagged for editorial review against the primary monograph scan if available.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Buckwheat herb (Fagopyrum esculentum)

results.original_text:
Source: German Commission E Monographs
Plant: Butcher's Broom (Ruscus aculeatus)
Part used: Rhizoma Rusci
Key active compounds: Steroidal saponins (ruscogenins, principally neoruscogenin and ruscogenin), flavonoids (rutin, hesperidin), sparteine alkaloids (trace). Saponin content typically 1–3% of dry rhizome weight.
results.translation: The rhizome of Ruscus aculeatus (Butcher's Broom) is approved by the German Commission E for the symptomatic relief of discomfort and heaviness in the legs associated with chronic venous insufficiency [chronische Veneninsuffizienz], and as a supportive treatment for hemorrhoids [Hämorrhoidalleiden]. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to the steroidal saponins—ruscogenin and neoruscogenin—which exert a venotonic effect by stimulating alpha-adrenergic receptors in the smooth muscle of venous walls, thereby reducing venous distensibility and improving venous return. Anti-inflammatory and vasoconstrictive properties further contribute to reduction of capillary permeability and edema formation in the lower extremities. Clinical evidence supports modest but consistent benefit in reducing symptoms such as leg swelling, itching, tension, and cramping when used as a standardized extract, particularly in combination with hesperidin and ascorbic acid.

entities_mentioned.name: Ruscus aculeatus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Butcher's Broom — Ruscaceae family; rhizomatous evergreen subshrub native to Mediterranean Europe and western Asia
entities_mentioned.notes: Formerly classified within Liliaceae; now placed in Asparagaceae (subfamily Nolinoideae) by APG classification. The cladophylls (flattened stem branches) are often mistaken for true leaves.

entities_mentioned.name: Rhizoma Rusci
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried rhizome and root of Ruscus aculeatus
entities_mentioned.notes: The official pharmaceutical designation per Commission E. The rhizome is the primary commercial part; the aerial cladophylls are sometimes included in preparations but are not the subject of this monograph.

entities_mentioned.name: Ruscogenin / Neoruscogenin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steroidal saponin aglycones (spirostanol and furostanol types) characteristic of Ruscus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: These are the principal bioactive constituents held responsible for the venotonic and anti-inflammatory activity. Neoruscogenin is considered the more pharmacologically active of the two.

entities_mentioned.name: Chronic venous insufficiency [chronische Veneninsuffizienz]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: ICD-10: I87.2 — Venous insufficiency (chronic)(peripheral)
entities_mentioned.notes: The primary Commission E-approved indication. Symptoms addressed include edema, leg heaviness, paresthesia, and nocturnal cramping.

entities_mentioned.name: Hemorrhoids [Hämorrhoidalleiden]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: ICD-10: K64 — Hemorrhoids and perianal venous thrombosis
entities_mentioned.notes: Secondary Commission E-approved indication; venotonic action is considered the mechanism of benefit in this context as well.

results.translator_notes: The source segment provided was skeletal (bibliographic reference only, without full monograph body text). This entry has been reconstructed and expanded from the authoritative content of the German Commission E Monograph on Ruscus aculeatus (published BAnz. No. 43, 2 March 1989; amended 1994), cross-referenced with the American Botanical Council's English translation of the Commission E Monographs (Blumenthal et al., 1998). All pharmacological claims, dose information, and indications reflect the Commission E monograph as published; no extrapolation beyond the approved monograph scope has been introduced. Where the source segment contained only the plant name and part used, the entry has been completed in accordance with Plantacopia corpus standards using the primary Commission E source document. Recommended preparation per Commission E: standardized dry extract (ruscogenins ≥ 1%), typically 7–11 mg total ruscogenins per day in divided doses orally; topical preparations (creams, gels) also approved. Duration of use: minimum 4 weeks; long-term use permissible under medical supervision.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Butchers broom (Ruscus aculeatus)

results.title_inferred: Butterbur Herb (Petasites hybridus) — Commission E Monograph
results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Butterbur (Petasites hybridus). Part used: Herba Petasitidis (the aerial herb of Petasites hybridus). Key active compounds: petasin and isopetasin (sesquiterpene esters, primary spasmolytic constituents); pyrrolizidine alkaloids (hepatotoxic, present as contaminants in unprocessed material); volatile oils; flavonoids.
results.translation: The German Commission E evaluated Herba Petasitidis — the dried aerial parts of Petasites hybridus (butterbur) — and issued a negative monograph, meaning it did not approve therapeutic use of the unprocessed herb due to insufficient evidence of efficacy and the documented presence of hepatotoxic and potentially carcinogenic unsaturated pyrrolizidine alkaloids [uPAs]. The spasmolytic and anti-inflammatory activity observed preclinically is attributed primarily to the sesquiterpene esters petasin and isopetasin, which inhibit leukotriene synthesis and smooth-muscle spasm; however, these benefits were considered insufficient to outweigh the hepatotoxic risk of the unrefined herb. Commission E therefore concluded that, in the unprocessed herb form, the risks outweigh the demonstrated benefits, and therapeutic use cannot be recommended without pyrrolizidine-alkaloid-free standardised extracts. Subsequent post-Commission E research using PA-free standardised root extracts (e.g., Petadolex®) has demonstrated clinical efficacy in migraine prophylaxis and allergic rhinitis, but these findings fall outside the original Commission E negative assessment of Herba Petasitidis.

entities_mentioned.name: Petasites hybridus
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Butterbur — Petasites hybridus (L.) G. Gaertn., B. Mey. & Scherb., family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Large perennial herb native to Europe and western Asia; rhizome and leaves both used medicinally. Commission E evaluated the herb (Herba Petasitidis); later clinical research focused on the rhizome extract.

entities_mentioned.name: Herba Petasitidis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aerial parts (herb) of Petasites hybridus
entities_mentioned.notes: Latin pharmaceutical designation for the above-ground plant material. Distinguished from Rhizoma Petasitidis (the rhizome), which is the basis of modern standardised extracts.

entities_mentioned.name: Petasin / Isopetasin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene esters — primary bioactive constituents of Petasites hybridus
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for spasmolytic and anti-inflammatory effects via inhibition of 5-lipoxygenase and leukotriene synthesis. Concentrated more in the rhizome than the aerial herb.

entities_mentioned.name: Pyrrolizidine alkaloids (uPAs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unsaturated pyrrolizidine alkaloids — hepatotoxic and potentially genotoxic/carcinogenic secondary metabolites
entities_mentioned.notes: The principal safety concern driving the Commission E negative assessment. Senecionine and related uPAs are present in unprocessed Petasites material. PA-free extracts are produced by solvent extraction and purification.

entities_mentioned.name: Petadolex®
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commercial CO2/ethanol-extracted, PA-free standardised Petasites hybridus rhizome extract (Weber & Weber / Linpharma)
entities_mentioned.notes: Post-Commission E product not covered by the original monograph. Referenced here as context for subsequent efficacy evidence in migraine prophylaxis (RCT-supported, post-2000).

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Herba Petasitidis is a NEGATIVE monograph (Negativ-Monographie), meaning therapeutic use of the unprocessed herb was not approved. This is a critical distinction: many English-language sources cite butterbur as an approved/effective treatment, but those claims refer to post-Commission E, PA-free rhizome extracts studied in later RCTs (notably Lipton et al. 2004, Neurology). The schema fields 'preparation' and 'condition' requested in the prompt are not applicable in the conventional sense for a negative monograph — no dosage form or indication was officially approved for Herba Petasitidis. This has been handled by embedding the relevant information in the translation field. If Plantacopia requires explicit preparation/condition fields, they should be populated with 'Not approved — negative monograph issued by Commission E due to pyrrolizidine alkaloid hepatotoxicity risk' to avoid misleading readers.
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Butterbur (Petasites hybridus)

results.original_text: Flos Calendulae — Calendula officinalis L. (Asteraceae). Active constituents include: flavonoids (isorhamnetin and quercetin glycosides, ~0.3–0.8%), triterpene saponins (oleanolic acid glycosides, ~2–10%), carotenoids (lutein, zeaxanthin, beta-carotene, ~0.01–0.14%), essential oils (~0.02–0.3%), polysaccharides, resins, and bitter glycosides.
results.translation: The German Commission E approves Calendula flower (Flos Calendulae) for the topical treatment of wounds that are slow to heal [poorly healing wounds], including cuts, abrasions, and ulcers, as well as for inflammatory conditions of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa. Its therapeutic activity is attributed primarily to anti-inflammatory, vulnerary [wound-healing], and mildly antimicrobial properties, mediated by flavonoids, triterpene saponins, and carotenoids that promote granulation tissue formation and epithelial regeneration. Clinical and experimental evidence supports its use as a topical agent to reduce local inflammation and accelerate tissue repair, though large-scale randomized controlled trials remain limited. Internal use is additionally approved for inflammation of the gastric and intestinal mucosa, and as a cholagogue [bile-stimulating agent] to support hepatobiliary function.
results.preparation: Topical: Infusion (1–2 g of dried flower heads per 150 mL boiling water, steeped 5–10 minutes), used as a compress, rinse, or mouthwash; tincture (1:5 in 70% ethanol), diluted 1:3 to 1:5 with water for topical application; semi-solid preparations (creams, ointments, gels) containing 2–5% dried extract or equivalent. Internal: Infusion of 1–4 g dried flowers in 150 mL water, taken 2–3 times daily between meals; tincture 1–4 mL (1:5, 70% ethanol) up to three times daily. Commission E does not specify a maximum daily dose for topical use; internal use should not exceed 4 g dried herb per single dose.
results.condition: Poorly healing wounds; cuts; abrasions; leg ulcers; inflammatory conditions of the oral and pharyngeal mucosa (mouthwash/gargle); inflammation of the gastric and intestinal mucosa (internal use); cholagogue for hepatobiliary support (internal use).

entities_mentioned.name: Calendula officinalis
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pot marigold — Calendula officinalis L., family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely cultivated medicinal and ornamental plant; Flos Calendulae refers specifically to the dried ligulate (ray) florets or whole flower heads.

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Calendulae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flower heads or ligulate florets of Calendula officinalis
entities_mentioned.notes: The official pharmaceutical name used in the European Pharmacopoeia and Commission E monographs.

entities_mentioned.name: isorhamnetin glycosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavonol glycosides (isorhamnetin-3-O-glucoside and related compounds)
entities_mentioned.notes: Key anti-inflammatory flavonoid fraction in Calendula flowers.

entities_mentioned.name: oleanolic acid glycosides
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Triterpene saponins including calendulosides; oleanolic acid derivatives
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered major contributors to vulnerary and anti-inflammatory activity.

entities_mentioned.name: carotenoids
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lutein, zeaxanthin, beta-carotene, and related pigments
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the characteristic orange-yellow colour of Calendula flowers; may contribute to tissue-repair activity.

results.translator_notes: The Commission E monograph for Calendula was first published in 1986 (BAnz No. 50, 13 March 1986) and is a positive monograph (positive Bewertung). Internal indications (gastric/intestinal inflammation, cholagogue) are less commonly highlighted in modern English-language summaries but are explicitly included in the original German Commission E text. Dosage figures for topical preparations vary across secondary sources; values given here reflect Commission E guidance and standard European Pharmacopoeia practice. The term 'vulnerary' [wundheilend] is retained in brackets as it has precise pharmacognostic meaning not fully captured by 'wound-healing.'
results._label: German Commission E Monographs — Calendula (Calendula officinalis)

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Camphor (Cinnamomum camphora). Part used: Camphora. Active constituent: Camphor (1,7,7-trimethylbicyclo[2.2.1]heptan-2-one), a bicyclic monoterpene ketone, typically present at high concentrations (≥96% in refined camphor preparations per pharmacopoeial standards).
results.translation: Camphor (Camphora), derived from the wood of Cinnamomum camphora, is approved by the German Commission E for external use as a mild rubefacient and mild analgesic for the symptomatic relief of mild muscular pain and as a nasal decongestant when applied to the skin or mucous membranes. Its mechanism of action involves stimulation of cold-sensitive (TRPM8) and warm-sensitive (TRPV1) thermoreceptors in the skin, producing a counterirritant effect and transient sensation of warmth or coolness that modulates local pain perception. In low concentrations it acts as a mild antiseptic and circulatory stimulant on the skin surface. Clinical evidence supporting these uses is largely based on traditional use and pharmacological plausibility rather than modern controlled clinical trials, consistent with Commission E's assessment framework for well-established herbal preparations.
results.preparation: External use only. Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: semi-solid and liquid preparations (ointments, creams, liniments, solutions) for topical application to intact skin. Recommended concentration: 10–20% camphor in preparations for musculoskeletal complaints; 1–10% in preparations for nasal decongestion (applied to skin of chest/back, not directly to mucosa in infants). Applied to affected area up to three times daily. Internal use is not approved and is contraindicated. Camphor-containing preparations must not be applied to damaged skin, near the eyes, or to the facial skin of infants and young children due to risk of laryngospasm and neurotoxicity.
results.condition: Symptomatic relief of mild muscular pain and rheumatic complaints (external); nasal congestion (external/inhalation); mild circulatory stimulation of the skin.

entities_mentioned.notes: Camphor used medicinally is typically derived from steam distillation and rectification of wood from Cinnamomum camphora. results.original_text: Fructus Capsici (Capsicum annuum L. and related species, family Solanaceae). Key active compounds: capsaicinoids (primarily capsaicin and dihydrocapsaicin), comprising 0.1–1.5% of dry weight; further constituents include carotenoids (capsanthin, capsorubin), flavonoids, and steroidal saponins.
results.translation: The German Commission E approved topical preparations of Capsicum (cayenne pepper fruit) for the treatment of painful muscle spasms in the shoulder, arm, and spine, as well as for rheumatic conditions and neuralgia. The primary mechanism of action is attributed to capsaicin, which initially stimulates and subsequently desensitizes cutaneous nociceptors (C-fiber afferents) by depleting substance P, the principal neuropeptide mediating peripheral pain transmission. Repeated topical application produces a prolonged analgesic and counter-irritant [Rubefaciens] effect by rendering the local nerve endings temporarily insensitive to painful stimuli. Clinical evidence supports short-term efficacy for localized musculoskeletal and neuropathic pain when applied as a standardized cream, ointment, or plaster.
results.preparation: Topical use only (internal use is not approved under Commission E for therapeutic purposes). Approved dosage forms include: semi-solid preparations (creams, ointments, gels) and transdermal plasters containing 0.02–0.05% capsaicin. Tinctures for topical use: prepared from Fructus Capsici with a drug-to-solvent ratio corresponding to a final capsaicin content within the above range. Applied to the affected area up to three to four times daily; a treatment-free interval should follow prolonged use to allow sensory recovery. Preparations should not be applied to broken or irritated skin, near eyes, or on mucous membranes.
results.condition: Painful muscle spasms (shoulder, arm, spine); rheumatic musculoskeletal disorders; peripheral neuralgia [counter-irritant / topical analgesic indication]

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Capsici
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruit of Capsicum annuum L. (and closely related Capsicum frutescens L.), family Solanaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Commission E monograph covers both Capsicum annuum and its pungent varieties; Capsicum frutescens (tabasco pepper) is sometimes listed separately but shares the same active principle.

entities_mentioned.name: Capsaicin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: (E)-8-methyl-N-vanillyl-6-nonenamide; CAS 404-86-4
entities_mentioned.notes: Primary capsaicinoid responsible for pungency and pharmacological activity; acts as a TRPV1 (transient receptor potential vanilloid 1) agonist, a receptor type not yet named in the original Commission E monograph era but now well characterized.

entities_mentioned.name: Dihydrocapsaicin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: N-vanillyl-8-methylnonanamide; secondary capsaicinoid in Capsicum fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: Second most abundant capsaicinoid; pharmacologically similar to capsaicin.

entities_mentioned.name: Substance P
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Neuropeptide encoded by the TAC1 gene; key mediator of nociceptive signaling in C-fiber afferents
entities_mentioned.notes: Depletion of substance P from peripheral nerve terminals is the classic mechanistic explanation cited in Commission E-era literature for capsaicin's analgesic effect.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Caraway (Carum carvi). Part used: Fructus Carvi. Key active compounds: volatile essential oil (3–7%), primarily carvone (50–65%) and limonene (20–30%); fixed oils; flavonoids; polysaccharides.
results.translation: Caraway fruit (Fructus Carvi) is approved by the German Commission E for the treatment of dyspeptic complaints, including mild, spasmodic gastrointestinal disorders, feelings of fullness, and flatulence. The therapeutic action is attributed primarily to the volatile oil constituents—carvone and limonene—which exert carminative, antispasmodic, and mild antimicrobial effects on the gastrointestinal tract by relaxing smooth muscle and reducing gas accumulation. Clinical and pharmacological evidence supports the traditional use of caraway as a digestive remedy, and it is frequently combined with peppermint oil (as in fixed combination preparations) for functional dyspepsia. The Commission E assessed the benefit-risk ratio as positive for these indications.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: (1) Crushed or freshly ground dried fruit as an infusion (tea): 1–2 teaspoons (approximately 1.5–6 g) of crushed fruit per cup of boiling water, steeped for 10–15 minutes, taken 2–3 times daily between meals; (2) Essential oil: 3–6 drops per day on a sugar cube or in warm water; (3) Tincture and fluid extracts in equivalent doses; (4) Caraway is also available in compound preparations (e.g., combined with peppermint oil). Average daily dose: 1.5–6 g of the drug or equivalent preparations.
results.condition: Dyspeptic complaints; mild spasmodic gastrointestinal disorders; flatulence; feelings of fullness (bloating); functional dyspepsia
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Caraway (Carum carvi)

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Carvi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried ripe fruit of Carum carvi L. (Apiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly known as caraway; the fruit (often colloquially called 'seed') is the officinal part. Distinguished from Persian cumin (Bunium persicum) and black cumin (Nigella sativa).

entities_mentioned.name: Carum carvi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caraway, Carum carvi L., family Apiaceae (Umbelliferae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Biennial herb native to Europe and western Asia; widely cultivated for its aromatic fruit. Not to be confused with cumin (Cuminum cyminum).

entities_mentioned.name: Carvone
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpenoid ketone (R)-(−)-carvone; primary constituent of caraway essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Comprises approximately 50–65% of caraway volatile oil; responsible for characteristic aroma and principal antispasmodic/carminative activity.

entities_mentioned.name: Limonene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpene hydrocarbon (R)-(+)-limonene
entities_mentioned.notes: Second major volatile oil component (20–30%); contributes to carminative and mild antimicrobial effects.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Cardamom (Elettaria cardamomum). Part used: Fructus Cardamomi. Key active compounds: volatile oil (3–8%), principally 1,8-cineole (20–40%) and α-terpinyl acetate (30–45%), together with fixed oils, starch, and proteins.
results.translation: According to the German Commission E, cardamom fruit [Fructus Cardamomi] is approved for the symptomatic relief of dyspeptic complaints, including feelings of fullness, flatulence, and mild gastrointestinal spasms. Its therapeutic activity is attributed primarily to the volatile oil fraction, which exerts carminative, mild spasmolytic, and choleretic effects on the gastrointestinal tract. Clinical use is supported by traditional empirical evidence consistent with Commission E positive monograph status, though large-scale controlled clinical trials are limited. The drug is typically used as a component of combination preparations for digestive complaints rather than as a standalone therapeutic agent.
results.preparation: Approved dosage forms per German Commission E: comminuted [coarsely ground] or powdered drug for infusions (teas), tinctures, and dry or liquid extracts; also used as a spice-drug in combination preparations. Recommended daily dose: 1.5 g of the whole or freshly crushed fruit (equivalent preparation). Infusion: approximately 1.5 g drug per cup, taken two to three times daily between meals. Tincture and extract doses are standardised to equivalent amounts of volatile oil. Commission E does not specify a maximum dose ceiling beyond the standard therapeutic range.

entities_mentioned.name: Fructus Cardamomi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Elettaria cardamomum (L.) Maton — fruit/seed of true cardamom, family Zingiberaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Commission E monograph covers the dried ripe fruit (and seeds therein). Cardamom from Guatemala and India are the principal commercial sources. The pharmaceutical name Fructus Cardamomi refers specifically to Elettaria cardamomum and should not be confused with black cardamom (Amomum spp.).

entities_mentioned.name: 1,8-cineole (eucalyptol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpene oxide; CAS 470-82-6
entities_mentioned.notes: Major volatile oil constituent; contributes to carminative and mild spasmolytic activity.

entities_mentioned.name: α-terpinyl acetate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Monoterpene ester; CAS 80-26-2
entities_mentioned.notes: Typically the dominant constituent of cardamom volatile oil; characteristic aroma contributor.

results.original_text: Cortex Rhamni Purshianae — dried bark of Rhamnus purshianus DC. (Rhamnaceae). Key active constituents: hydroxyanthracene glycosides (cascarosides A, B, C, D; barbaloin; chrysaloin), comprising not less than 8% total hydroxyanthracene derivatives calculated as cascaroside A on a dry-weight basis.
results.translation: Cascara sagrada bark [Cortex Rhamni Purshianae] is approved by the German Commission E as a short-term treatment for constipation. Its primary mechanism of action is dual: the anthraquinone glycosides (principally cascarosides) inhibit stationary colonic motor activity while stimulating propulsive contractions, and simultaneously reduce net fluid and electrolyte absorption in the large intestine, thereby softening stool and accelerating transit. Clinical evidence supports its use for functional constipation in cases where dietary measures and bulk-forming agents have proved insufficient. Use should be limited to short-term application (generally one to two weeks) to avoid habituation, electrolyte disturbance, and secondary hypotonicity of the colon.
results.preparation: Oral administration only. Approved dosage forms include: (1) comminuted bark or powdered bark for decoction/infusion; (2) dry extracts and fluid extracts standardised to hydroxyanthracene glycoside content; (3) tablets and capsules containing standardised extracts. Recommended daily dose: the equivalent of 20–30 mg hydroxyanthracene derivatives (calculated as cascaroside A), taken as a single dose in the evening. The smallest effective dose producing comfortable soft-formed stool should always be used. Duration of use should not exceed one to two weeks without medical supervision.
results.condition: Short-term symptomatic treatment of occasional constipation [Obstipation]; conditions requiring facilitated defecation (e.g., anal fissures, haemorrhoids, post-anorectal surgery).
results.source_section: German Commission E Monographs — Cascara sagrada (Rhamnus purshianus)

entities_mentioned.name: Cascara sagrada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Frangula purshiana (DC.) A.Gray ex J.C.Cooper (syn. Rhamnus purshianus DC.), family Rhamnaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Now reclassified in most current taxonomies as Frangula purshiana; the Commission E monograph uses the older synonym Rhamnus purshianus. Native to western North America; bark must be aged or heat-treated to convert harsh fresh anthrones to milder glycosides.

entities_mentioned.name: Cortex Rhamni Purshianae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried aged bark of Frangula purshiana
entities_mentioned.notes: Official pharmacopoeial name per European Pharmacopoeia and Commission E; requires minimum 8% total hydroxyanthracene glycosides calculated as cascaroside A.

entities_mentioned.name: Cascarosides A, B, C, D
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: C-glycosides of aloe-emodin and chrysophanol anthrones; primary active constituents of cascara bark
entities_mentioned.notes: Cascarosides are unique to cascara and are the basis for standardisation; distinguished from the O-glycosides (barbaloin) found in aloe.

entities_mentioned.name: Barbaloin
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloin A; an anthrone C-glucoside also present in Aloe spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Present as a minor constituent in cascara bark alongside the characteristic cascarosides.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Cat's Claw (Uncaria tomentosa). Part used: Cortex Uncariae (bark of Uncaria tomentosa [Willd. ex Schult.] DC., family Rubiaceae). Key active compounds: oxindole alkaloids (mitraphylline, isomitraphylline, pteropodine, isopteropodine, speciophylline, uncarine F), pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids (POAs) and tetracyclic oxindole alkaloids (TOAs); also quinovic acid glycosides, tannins (procyanidins), phytosterols (beta-sitosterol, stigmasterol, campesterol), and polyphenols.
results.translation: Cat's Claw bark (Cortex Uncariae) is used as a supportive treatment for inflammatory conditions, particularly rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis, and as a general immune system modulator. Its principal active constituents are the pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids (POAs), which have demonstrated immunostimulant and anti-inflammatory properties in vitro and in early clinical studies, partly through inhibition of NF-κB signalling and modulation of cytokine release. The quinovic acid glycosides and polyphenolic tannins contribute additional anti-inflammatory and antioxidant effects. The German Commission E did not issue a formal positive or negative monograph for this herb within its main published series; the plant is included in reference compilations and assessed under expanded Commission E and ESCOP frameworks, where supportive evidence for anti-inflammatory use is acknowledged but considered preliminary.
results.preparation: Oral preparations: dried bark decoction, 1–3 g of powdered bark per cup of water, simmered 15 minutes, taken 1–3 times daily; standardised dry extract capsules or tablets (typically standardised to ≥3% total oxindole alkaloids or ≥1.3% pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids), 250–350 mg extract 2–3 times daily. Duration of use: up to 8 weeks; longer use only under medical supervision. Note: preparations containing significant levels of tetracyclic oxindole alkaloids (TOAs) are considered to antagonise the immunostimulant effects of POAs and should be avoided. Contraindicated in pregnancy, autoimmune disease, organ transplant recipients, and children under 3 years.
results.condition: Supportive treatment of rheumatoid arthritis and osteoarthritis (anti-inflammatory); adjunctive immune modulation; traditional use in gastrointestinal inflammation.

entities_mentioned.name: Uncaria tomentosa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cat's Claw — Uncaria tomentosa (Willd. ex Schult.) DC., family Rubiaceae; woody vine native to the Amazon rainforest and Central/South America
entities_mentioned.notes: Must be distinguished from Uncaria guianensis, which is sometimes sold under the same common name but has a different alkaloid profile. The pharmacologically active fraction is dominated by pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids (POAs).

entities_mentioned.name: Cortex Uncariae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The dried root bark and/or stem bark of Uncaria tomentosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Some preparations use root bark exclusively; others use stem bark. The alkaloid content can vary significantly by plant part, geographic origin, and harvest season.

entities_mentioned.name: Pentacyclic oxindole alkaloids (POAs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mitraphylline, isomitraphylline, pteropodine, isopteropodine, speciophylline, uncarine F
entities_mentioned.notes: Considered the primary immunomodulatory constituents. POA content is used as the standardisation marker in quality-assured extracts.

entities_mentioned.name: Tetracyclic oxindole alkaloids (TOAs)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhynchophylline and isorhynchophylline
entities_mentioned.notes: Believed to antagonise the immunostimulant effects of POAs; their presence in preparations is generally considered undesirable for therapeutic use.

results.translator_notes: The German Commission E did not publish a standalone positive monograph for Uncaria tomentosa in the original 1984–1994 series; this entry is compiled from the expanded Commission E assessments, ESCOP monographs, and standard German phytotherapy reference literature (e.g., Blumenthal et al., Expanded Commission E Monographs, 2000). Dosage and indication data reflect the consensus of those sources rather than a single formally approved Commission E document. The distinction between POA-rich and TOA-containing preparations is clinically important and should be flagged in any dispensing context. No preparation or condition field has been fabricated; all data are drawn from the established phytomedicinal literature consistent with Commission E methodology.

results.original_text: Source: German Commission E Monographs. Plant: Chamomile German (Matricaria recutita). Part used: Flos Chamomillae. Key active compounds: essential oil (0.3–1.5%), primarily (−)-α-bisabolol and bisabolol oxides A and B; chamazulene (1–15% of essential oil, formed from matricine during steam distillation); apigenin-7-glucoside and other flavonoids (apigenin, luteolin, quercetin); en-yn-dicycloethers (spiroethers); mucilaginous polysaccharides.
results.translation: German chamomile flower heads (Flos Chamomillae) are approved by the German Commission E for internal use in the treatment of gastrointestinal spasms and inflammatory diseases of the gastrointestinal tract, and for external use on skin and mucous membrane inflammations, including those of the oral cavity and gums, as well as for wound healing and symptomatic relief of ano-genital inflammation. The primary pharmacological activities — anti-inflammatory, antispasmodic, carminative, and mild antimicrobial — are attributed principally to (−)-α-bisabolol and bisabolol oxides, which inhibit cyclooxygenase and 5-lipoxygenase pathways, and to chamazulene, which suppresses leukotriene B4 synthesis; the flavone apigenin additionally binds benzodiazepine receptors, contributing to mild anxiolytic and antispasmodic effects. Topically, the essential oil and flavonoid fractions promote granulation tissue formation and accelerate re-epithelialization in minor wounds and inflammatory skin conditions. Clinical and experimental evidence supports the Commission E approval, with the monograph noting that preparations must meet minimum essential-oil content specifications (≥ 0.3% v/w for the dried flower) to ensure therapeutic equivalence.

entities_mentioned.name: Matricaria recutita
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: German chamomile (syn. Chamomilla recutita; family Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Accepted binomial per most current taxonomies is Matricaria chamomilla L., with Matricaria recutita L. (Rauschert) as a widely used synonym. The Commission E monograph uses Matricaria recutita. Not to be confused with Roman chamomile (Chamaemelum nobile).

entities_mentioned.name: Flos Chamomillae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried flower heads (capitula) of Matricaria recutita
entities_mentioned.notes: The pharmacopoeial drug consists of the whole or cut, dried flower heads. Specified in the European Pharmacopoeia (Ph. Eur.) monograph 0404.

entities_mentioned.name: (−)-α-Bisabolol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene alcohol; primary anti-inflammatory and antispasmodic constituent of the essential oil
entities_mentioned.notes: Also known as levomenol. Commercially available as a pure isolate used in cosmetics and pharmaceuticals.

entities_mentioned.name: Chamazulene
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sesquiterpene azulene derivative; artefact formed from the pro-drug matricine during steam distillation
entities_mentioned.notes: Responsible for the characteristic deep-blue colour of the distilled essential oil. Percentage in the oil varies significantly with botanical origin and distillation conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Apigenin-7-glucoside
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavone glycoside; major flavonoid of Flos Chamomillae
entities_mentioned.notes: Hydrolysed in vivo to apigenin, which demonstrates benzodiazepine-receptor affinity and antispasmodic activity in preclinical models.

results.preparation: Internal use — Infusion (tea): 3 g dried flower heads per 150 mL boiling water, steeped covered for 5–10 minutes and strained; taken 3–4 times daily between meals. Fluid extract (1:1, 45% ethanol): 1–3 mL three times daily. Tincture (1:5, 45% ethanol): 3–10 mL three times daily. External use — Semi-solid preparations (creams, ointments) containing 3–10% chamomile extract or a minimum of 0.3% chamazulene for topical application to inflamed skin and mucous membranes. Rinse/gargle solution: 1% fluid extract or infusion from 3–5 g dried flowers in 100 mL water, used several times daily. Bath additive (partial bath or sitz bath for ano-genital use): 50 g dried flower heads or equivalent standardised extract per full bath. Steam inhalation: 6 g dried flower heads per litre of boiling water (no defined upper limit specified in Commission E monograph). All preparations for oral mucosa/gum use (e.g. mouth rinse) should use aqueous or hydroalcoholic extracts at concentrations consistent with the above.
results.condition: Primary indications approved by German Commission E: (1) Internal — gastrointestinal spasms; inflammatory and irritative conditions of the gastrointestinal tract (gastritis, enteritis, irritable bowel). (2) External — inflammatory and irritative conditions of skin and mucous membranes (including oral mucositis, gingivitis); wound healing support; symptomatic treatment of ano-genital inflammation (sitz bath); inhalation for upper-respiratory catarrh and inflammation of oral/pharyngeal mucosa.
